coredata.dc:title: Room temperature driven highly crystalline fluorine-doped carbon quantum dots for sensitive tetracycline sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Yiqian
Huang, Xiaosong
Lin, Huashan
Liu, Zhikun
Zong, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with semiconductor-like properties have received considerable attention because of their unique photoelectric properties and electron transfer ability. However, the preparation of highly crystalline CQDs with strong fluorescence (FL) emission by the room temperature (RT) synthesis is a challenging task. Herein, we develop a highly-efficient RT synthesis strategy based on the Schiff base condensation reaction for preparing the fluorine-doped CQDs (F-CQDs) with good crystallinity. The as-prepared F-CQDs show strong blue FL emission with a high quantum yield of 39%, which is the highest values yet recorded for carbon dots prepared by RT synthesis. Moreover, the F-CQDs can be used for sensitive tetracycline sensing based on inner filter effect. The FL intensity ratio (F 0/F) of the F-CQDs is proportional to the concentration of tetracycline in the range of 0.1–25 μM with the limit of detection of 85 nM. This sensitive assay has a good application prospect for tetracycline detection in the complex matrixes because of its simplicity and sensitivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorine-doped carbon quantum dots
Room temperature synthesis
Tetracycline detection
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and purification of the F-CQDs and CQDs
FL sensing of tetracycline using the F-CQDs
Tetracycline detection using the as-prepared F-CQDs
Analysis of tetracycline in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and structural characterization of the F-CQDs. (a) TEM image (inset: size distribution); (b) HRTEM image; (c) Raman spectrum; (d) FT-IR spectrum; (e) XPS spectrum; (f) C1s spectrum; (g) N1s spectrum and (h) O1s spectrum of the F-CQDs.
The optical properties of the F-CQDs. The (a) absorption spectrum, (b) emission spectrum, (c) 3D FL spectrum and (d) FL lifetime of the F-CQDs.

The FL quenching of the F-CQDs by tetracycline. (a) FL intensities of the F-CQDs before and after adding 1 mM tetracycline. λ ex: 360 nm. (b) The sensitivity of tetracycline detection. pH: 7.4; λ ex: 360 nm. F0 and F are the FL intensity in the absence and presence of tetracycline, respectively. (c) Effect of other antibiotics on the FL of the F-CQDs. The concentration of the antibiotics is 100 μM. The concentration of the tetracycline is 5 μM. (d) Effect of metal ions on the FL of the F-CQDs. The columns represent: 1: control; 2: Ca2+; 3: Ba2+; 4: Al3+; 5: Cd2+; 6: Cr3+; 7: Fe3+; 8: Pb2+; 9: Mn2+; 10: Mg2+; 11: Li+; 12: Na+; 13: Ni2+; 14: Hg2+; 15: Zn2+. The concentration of the tetracycline is 5 μM. The concentration of the metal ions is 100 μΜ. pH: 7.4; λ ex: 360 nm.

The detection mechanism of the F-CQDs. (a) Absorption spectra of antibiotic and the excitation and emission spectra of the F-CQDs. (b) The FL delays of the F-CQDs in the absence and presence of tetracycline (1 μM).
Illustration of the synthesis process of the F-CQDs by one-pot RT synthesis and the highly specific detection of tetracycline by the IFE.
Comparison of the characterization of F-CQDs obtained in our experiment with CDs prepared by different RT synthesis in the literatures.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a typical excitation-dependent emission behaviour (Fig. 2c). The excitation-dependent FL may be due to the inhomogeneous size distribution [44,45]. Greatly differ from the F-CQDs, the undoped CQDs emit an excitation-independent green FL (Fig. S3). Because of the strong electronegativity of fluorine atom, the blue-shifted emission caused by the fluorine doping indicates that the introduction of fluorine can change the electron density and adjust the optical properties of CQDs [1].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of tetracycline in real samples. aAverage of three replicate measurements.

head.ce:title: Room temperature driven highly crystalline fluorine-doped carbon quantum dots for sensitive tetracycline sensing

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with semiconductor-like properties have received considerable attention because of their unique photoelectric properties and electron transfer ability. However, the preparation of highly crystalline CQDs with strong fluorescence (FL) emission by the room temperature (RT) synthesis is a challenging task. Herein, we develop a highly-efficient RT synthesis strategy based on the Schiff base condensation reaction for preparing the fluorine-doped CQDs (F-CQDs) with good crystallinity. The as-prepared F-CQDs show strong blue FL emission with a high quantum yield of 39%, which is the highest values yet recorded for carbon dots prepared by RT synthesis. Moreover, the F-CQDs can be used for sensitive tetracycline sensing based on inner filter effect. The FL intensity ratio (F 0/F) of the F-CQDs is proportional to the concentration of tetracycline in the range of 0.1–25 μM with the limit of detection of 85 nM. This sensitive assay has a good application prospect for tetracycline detection in the complex matrixes because of its simplicity and sensitivity.

Herein, highly crystalline fluorine-doped carbon quantum dots are prepared by the room temperature synthesis strategy for the sensitive detection of tetracycline by the strong inner filter effect.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), an important member of carbon dots (CDs) family [1], have received continuous attention recently because of their various fascinating properties such as high photostability, good biocompatibility, tunable fluorescence (FL) and excellent electronic properties [2–6]. As a new type of fluorescent nanoprobe, CQDs have been widely used to chemo/biosensing [7–10], cellular imaging [11,12], drug delivery [13–15] and light emitting devices (LEDs) [16–19]. Spherical CQDs with quantum confinement are considered as a core-shell carbon nanostructure, which contains nanocrystalline carbon core consisting of multiple layers of graphitic sheets and carbon shell consisting of surface functional groups [20].

The formation of crystalline carbon core of CQDs usually needs the carbonization of organic molecules at high temperature. The reaction temperature is required to be higher than the melting point of organic molecules, usually about 200 °C [21]. Such high temperature not only needs a lot of energy support, but also may produce other side reactions in the process of CQDs formation, leading to the heterogeneity of CQDs components. Room temperature (RT) synthesis provides a powerful strategy for preparing crystalline CQDs without heating equipment and external energy supply. Importantly, RT synthesis can avoid many side reactions such as oxidation that often occur at high temperature.

Although RT synthesis has many advantages, the CQDs prepared by the most RT synthesis strategies have low luminescent efficiency (quantum yield (QY) < 5%) [22,23], and complex post-processing [24–26]. To date, common RT synthesis can be achieved by chemical oxidation of large carbon materials such as activated carbon and carbon soot. But the as-formed CQDs have low crystallinity, and even contain a lot of amorphous polymers. Therefore, developing a RT synthesis strategy for preparing highly fluorescent CQDs with high crystallinity is very desired.

EDA and tetracycline were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). TFBQ was obtained from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents were used as received without further purification.

The TEM images were performed on a Tecnai G2 F20 field emission transmission electron microscope. The absorption spectrum was obtained from a Hitachi U-3010 spectrophotometer. The FL spectra were measured by a Hitachi F-2500 FL spectrophotometer. The element compositions were analysed using an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The FT-IR spectrum was collected on a Hitachi FTIR-8400S Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and purification of the F-CQDs and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The F-CQDs could be synthesized by a facile RT synthesis strategy. To put it crudely, 0.01 g TFBQ was dissolved with 4.7 mL water, and then mixed with 0.3 mL EDA. After mixing, the reaction solution was kept for 48 h. Finally, dark brown F-CQDs solution with strong FL was obtained. Residual amounts of EDA and TFBQ were removed by a cellulose ester dialysis membrane (100–500 MWCO) over 48 h. The resulting product was dried by lyophilization to obtain F-CQDs powder, which were dispersed in water for further use. For comparison, the undoped CQDs are prepared using benzoquinone (BQ) and EDA based on the above similar experiment process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FL sensing of tetracycline using the F-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of tetracycline, the F-CQDs solution is mixed with the various concentrations of tetracycline (0 − 100 μM). After 5 min, the FL spectra of the mixture solution are measured. To evaluate selectivity of the sensor for tetracycline, different interferents including other antibiotics and metal ions are added under the same experimental conditions.

RT synthesis strategy based on the Schiff base condensation reaction can be well used for the preparation of F-CQDs with high crystallinity. TEM image (Fig. 1 a) displays that the size of the F-CQDs is distributed in the range from 4 to 9 nm with an average particle size of 6.5 nm. HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) further shows that the F-CQDs prepared by using this RT strategy have high crystallinity with lattice fringe spacing of 0.215 nm, which can be ascribed to the (100) lattice spacing of graphene [1]. The Raman spectrum (Fig. 1c) of the F-CQDs shows that the D-band at 1384 cm−1 is due to disordered sp [3]-hybridized carbon, while the G-band at 1599 cm−1 is due to ordered sp [2]-hybridized carbon. The small intensity ratio (ID/IG) of 0.89 further indicates that the structure of the F-CQDs is highly crystalline with an ordered graphite-like structure [1]. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1d) of the F-CQDs shows two characteristic absorption bands of N–H and CN stretching vibrations at 3450 cm−1 and 1642 cm−1, respectively, indicating that the Schiff base condensation occurs in the synthesis of F-CQDs [31–34]. For comparison, the undoped CQDs are prepared using BQ and EDA. Differ from the F-CQDs, the crystallinity of undoped CQDs is obviously poorer than that of the F-CQDs (Fig. S1). This may be because Michael addition reaction can occur between BQ and EDA, besides Schiff base condensation [35,36]. More reaction paths may make the intrinsic structure of CQDs more complex and disordered. Moreover, XRD spectra (Fig. S2) further reveal that the doping of fluorine improves the crystallinity of CQDs. The CQDs have a weak and broad diffraction peak from 10° to 40° corresponds to the amorphous structure, while a relatively sharp peak near 23° appeared in F-CQDs, which is attributed to the graphite-like multilayer structure [37].

XPS spectrum is used to further analyze the element composition of the F-CQDs, which shows that the F-CQDs have C1s, N1s, O1s and F1s peaks located at about 285.4 eV, 398.9 eV, 531.1 eV and 686.8 eV, respectively (Fig. 1e). The proportions of C, N, O, and F elements are 60.9%, 18.5%, 14.5% and 6.1%, respectively (Table S1). The C1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) displays four peaks at 284.4 eV, 285.2 eV, 285.8 eV and 287.3 eV, which can be ascribed to the C–C/CC, C–N, C–O and CN/CO bonds, respectively [20]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1g) shows two peaks at 398.8 eV and 399.8 eV, which are attributed to the C–N and CN bonds, respectively [38]. Moreover, the O1s spectrum (Fig. 1h) shows two peaks at 530.8 eV and 531.9 eV, which are attributed to the C–OH and CO bonds, respectively [39].

Because of the π–π* transition of aromatic CC bonds and the n–π* transition of CN bonds, the as-formed F-CQDs display two characteristic absorptions at about 269 nm and 390 nm (Fig. 2 a) [40]. Fig. 2b shows the FL spectra of the F-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths. The FL emission peak of the F-CQDs will shift to the longer wavelength when the excitation wavelength increases from 300 nm to 450 nm. The maximum FL emission peak located at 445 nm when the excitation at 360 nm, giving.

The F-CQDs show a typical nanosecond lifetime of 4.40(Fig. 2d). The short lifetime reveals the radiative recombination nature of excitations in F-CQDs, which indicates that the F-CQDs can be well used for analytical and biological applications [46]. The absolute QY of the F-CQDs reaches 39%, which is the highest values yet recorded for CDs prepared by RT synthesis (Table 1 ). Meanwhile, the QY of the F-CQDs is also higher than that of the undoped CQDs (15%), further revealing that fluorine doping can well improve the fluorescent efficiency of CQDs. Combining the FL lifetime, the radiative rate of the F-CQDs is 8.86 × 107 s−1 based on this following formula [47]:
k r = φ / τ
wherein k r refers to the radiative rate constant, while φ and τ refer to the QY and FL lifetime, respectively. Such high radiative rate indicates that high electronic transition may exist in the F-CQDs [48], which attributes to the high luminescence efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tetracycline detection using the as-prepared F-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline can be used to efficiently quench the FL of the F-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the blue FL of the F-CQDs is almost completely quenched when adding 1 mM tetracycline. To test the sensitivity of the F-CQDs probe, a series of different concentrations of tetracycline in the range of 0–100 μM have been investigated. With the increase of tetracycline concentration, the FL of the F-CQDs decrease rapidly, indicating the sensitivity of this developed detection method to tetracycline concentration. The FL intensity ratio (F 0/F) of the F-CQDs is proportional to the concentration of tetracycline in the range of 0.1 − 25 μM with a low LOD of 85 nM (3σ/k) (Fig. 3b). Other CDs-based detection methods are used to compare with our method (Table S2), although the LOD of the proposed one is not the lowest, the simple preparation (RT synthesis), high quantum yield (39%), and high sensitivity (85 nM) make the prepared F-CQDs have great potential in the application of tetracycline detection.
Furthermore, we investigate the specificity of the assay toward tetracycline over some common interferences, including other antibiotics and metal ions. As shown in.

Fig. 3c, this developed sensing system is highly-selective toward tetracycline over the tested antibiotics. The common antibiotics including streptomycin, kanamycin, penicllin, procaine, and cefotaxime have no effect on the FL of the F-CQDs. Besides, the other interferents such as metal ions also have no influence on the FL of the F-CQDs in the absence and presence of tetracycline (Fig. S4 and Fig. 3d), indicating that these metal ions will not interfere with the detection of tetracycline. Moreover, the change of polarity by introducing different solvents in the detection system has no effect on the detection of tetracycline (Fig. S5). Meanwhile, another advantage of our sensing system is fast detection, and the FL intensity of the F-CQDs can be determined after adding tetracycline.

The FL quenching of fluorescent probes in the sensing system is closely related with the charge transfer or inner filter effect (IFE) between probe and analyze [5,32,49–51]. To reveal if there is a charge transfer process between F-CQDs and tetracycline, we estimate their energy levels of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (E LUMO) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (E HOMO). The E LUMO and E HOMO of the F-CQDs are estimated as −4.02 and −8.19 eV, respectively (Figs. S6a and b). Moreover, the E LUMO and E HOMO of tetracycline are calculated as −2.26 eV and −6.00 eV, respectively, based on the previous report [30]. As shown in Fig. S6c, photoexcited electrons in F-CQDs cannot be transferred to the LUMO of the tetracycline, indicating that the charge transfer process between F-CQDs and tetracycline is impossible. While the IFE only requires a good spectral overlap between the excitation or emission band of the fluorescent probes and absorption band of the analyze [5]. The analyze can competitively absorb the excitation or emission light of the fluorescent probes, causing the FL quenching of fluorescent probes.

Based on the above discussion, the special FL quenching of the F-CQDs by tetracycline is directly attributed to the strong IFE between the F-CQDs and tetracycline. The tetracycline shows a strong absorption band at about 358 nm, and has a great spectral overlap with the excitation spectrum of the F-CQDs (Fig. S7a). While the tetracycline has a smaller spectral overlap with the excitation spectrum of the CQDs as compared to the F-CQDs (Fig. S7b), indicating that the tetracycline can more effectively compete to absorb the excitation light of the F-CQDs. This result also reveals that the detection efficiency of F-CQDs will be higher than that of undoped CQDs. On the contrary, the absorption spectra of other types of antibiotics hardly overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of the F-CQDs (Fig. 4 a). Thus they have no effect on the FL of the F-CQDs in the detection of tetracycline. In addition, the unchanged FL decays (Fig. 4b) of the F-CQDs in the absence and presence of tetracycline further indicate strong IFE occurring between F-CQDs and tetracycline.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of tetracycline in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: This developed sensing strategy can be used for the detection of tetracyclin in different real samples including water, milk and blood samples. Three concentrations of standard tetracyclin solutions (0.1, 1, and 5 μM) are added into the F-CQDs solution as listed in Table 2 . The recovery study is used for further analysing the reliability and accuracy of the sensing method we constructed. The recoveries of three samples varied from 89.1% to 113.4%, indicating that this strategy has a good application prospect in tetracyclin detection because of its simplicity and specificity.

In conclusion, a facile and efficient RT synthesis strategy is developed for the preparation of the F-CQDs with high crystallinity based on the Schiff base condensation reaction. Importantly, the F-CQDs show a strong blue FL with a high QY of about 39%, which is the highest value recorded for CDs prepared by RT synthesis. Meanwhile, the F-CQDs probes can be used for the sensitive and selective detection of tetracycline in the range of 0.1 – 25 μM with the LODs of 85 nM (3σ/k). Besides, the F-CQDs have high reproducibility for detecting tetracycline in different real samples.

Yiqian Huang: Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xiaosong Huang: Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Huashan Lin: Visualization, Investigation. Zhikun Liu: Visualization, Validation. Yi Zong: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81802289), Medical Scientific Research Foundation of Zhejiang Province (2018RC082) and Science and Technology key program of Jinhua Science and Technology Bureau (2020-03-009).
